name: Bhaktisiddhanta Saraswati
dates_notes: Born 6 February 1874 in Puri, Odisha; died 1 January 1937. Son of Bhaktivinoda Ṭhākura. Founder of the Gauḍīya Maṭha (1920). Dīkṣā from Gaurakiśora Dāsa Bābājī.

dates_evidence.description: Caitanya Maṭha and Gauḍīya Maṭha institutional records.

core_thesis: Bhaktisiddhānta Sarasvatī, son of Bhaktivinoda Ṭhākura and founder of the Gauḍīya Maṭha (1920), is the figure through whom Caitanya's *acintya-bhedābheda* receives its twentieth-century institutional and commentarial reformulation. His *Anubhāṣya* on the *Caitanya-Caritāmṛta* (1915) is the principal modern doctrinal commentary on Kṛṣṇadāsa Kavirāja's biography (Bhaktisiddhānta, *Anubhāṣya*, Caitanya Maṭha 1915 edition, Introduction), articulating *acintya-śakti* as the load-bearing concept of Gauḍīya metaphysics (Bhaktisiddhānta, *Anubhāṣya* on *Caitanya-Caritāmṛta* Ādi-līlā 1.51–58 and 4.62–67, on simultaneous identity-and-difference of Bhagavān, *jīva*, and world). His Sanskrit and Bengali commentary on the *Bhāgavata-Purāṇa* (serialised 1923–1935 in *Sajjana-Toṣaṇī* and *Gauḍīya*) re-reads the text through the *Ṣaṭ-Sandarbha* of Jīva Gosvāmī (cf. Bhaktisiddhānta, *Bhāgavata*-comm. on 1.1.1, with reference to Jīva, *Tattva-Sandarbha* §§ 27–34). His *Brahma-Saṃhitā* commentary fixes the school's modern reading of that pre-modern hymn (Bhaktisiddhānta, comm. on *Brahma-Saṃhitā* 5.1, *īśvaraḥ paramaḥ kṛṣṇaḥ*; 5.29–35, the *cintāmaṇi-prakara* and Govinda-Goloka verses). Three distinctive emphases run through the work. First, explicit polemic against Advaita as a deviation (*Anubhāṣya* on *CC* Madhya-līlā 6.166–262, the Caitanya-Sārvabhauma debate). Second, institutional codification of *vaiṣṇava-dīkṣā* outside hereditary lineages, against the *jāti-vaiṣṇava* practice of the late-nineteenth-century Bengali sahajiyā milieu (*Vaiṣṇava-Siddhānta-Mālā*, sections on *dīkṣā-tattva* and *guru-tattva*). Third, vigorous use of print-publication as the *bṛhad-mṛdaṅga* — the great drum of Hari-saṅkīrtana (Bhaktisiddhānta, editorials in *Gauḍīya*, 1922–1935; cf. *Vaiṣṇava-Siddhānta-Mālā*, section on *prachāra*). His direct disciple A. C. Bhaktivedānta Swāmī Prabhupāda extends the lineage globally.

engaged_works.title: Anubhashya on Caitanya-Caritamrita
engaged_works.summary: A Bengali commentary on Kṛṣṇadāsa Kavirāja's *Caitanya-Caritāmṛta*, the school's canonical biography of Caitanya. The commentary is the principal modern doctrinal reading of the *Caitanya-Caritāmṛta*, and articulates three load-bearing positions. First, *acintya-śakti* is the Lord's intrinsic power by which simultaneous identity-and-difference of Bhagavān, *jīva*, and world is grounded; the *acintya* qualifier is not an evasion of logical difficulty but a registration of the structural inadequacy of finite logic to the *parātpara* relation (*Anubhāṣya* on *CC* Ādi-līlā 1.51–58 and 7.117–124). Second, *prema-bhakti* is the *parā* mode and the proper end of the *jīva*'s existence; *jñāna-mokṣa* and *karma-lokottara* are subordinate (*Anubhāṣya* on *CC* Madhya-līlā 8.66–95, the Rāmānanda Rāya samvāda; Madhya 22.5–164, the *bhakti-tattva* exposition). Third, the *Caitanya-Caritāmṛta* is on a par with *Veda*, *Brahma-Sūtra*, and *Bhāgavata* as authority for the school (*Anubhāṣya* on *CC* Ādi-līlā 2.117 and Antya-līlā 5.85–88). The work fixes the modern Gauḍīya reading and is the natural starting point for any twentieth- or twenty-first-century engagement with the school's doctrine.

engaged_works.title: Bengali-Sanskrit Commentary on Bhagavata-Purana
engaged_works.composition_dates_notes: Serialised in Sajjana-Toṣaṇī and the Gauḍīya, 1923–1935.
engaged_works.summary: A Bengali commentary with Sanskrit doctrinal apparatus on the *Bhāgavata-Purāṇa*. The reading is constructed through the *Ṣaṭ-Sandarbha* of Jīva Gosvāmī: the *Tattva*-, *Bhagavat*-, *Paramātmā*-, *Kṛṣṇa*-, *Bhakti*-, and *Prīti-Sandarbha* are taken as the school's canonical interpretive grid, and the *Bhāgavata*'s loci are read at each contested point through the relevant *Sandarbha* (Bhaktisiddhānta, *Bhāgavata*-comm. on 1.1.1, 1.2.11, 10.1.1, with cross-references to Jīva, *Tattva-Sandarbha* §§ 27–34 and *Kṛṣṇa-Sandarbha* §§ 28–29 *kṛṣṇas tu bhagavān svayam*). The book-10 *līlā* material (*Bhāg.* 10.21–33, the *veṇu-* through *rāsa-pañcādhyāyī*) receives the school's modern devotional theology; the cosmogonic material of book 3 (*Bhāg.* 3.5–7, 3.26–32 the Sāṅkhya material) is read against Sāṅkhya-evolutionary alternatives; the *Uddhava-Gītā* of book 11 (*Bhāg.* 11.6–29) is treated as the practical-pedagogical complement to the Bhagavad-Gītā. The commentary is the modern Gauḍīya reference for *Bhāgavata*-exegesis and the principal documentary source for the school's twentieth-century doctrinal continuity.

engaged_works.title: Commentary on Brahma-Samhita
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored Bengali-Sanskrit commentary on the fifth chapter of the Brahma-Saṃhitā.
engaged_works.summary: A commentary on the fifth *adhyāya* of the *Brahma-Saṃhitā*, the cosmological hymn the school treats as the Brahmā-revealed scripture on the supreme Person. The doctrinal payoff is the school's reading of *Govinda*-Kṛṣṇa as the *parātpara* source from which all *avatāra*-modes proceed (Bhaktisiddhānta, comm. on *Brahma-Saṃhitā* 5.1, *īśvaraḥ paramaḥ kṛṣṇaḥ sac-cid-ānanda-vigrahaḥ*; 5.39, *rāmādi-mūrtiṣu kalā-niyamena tiṣṭhan*), and of the cosmological hierarchy — Goloka, Vaikuṇṭha, the *brahma-jyoti*, the *vyūha*-emanations — that follows (comm. on *BS* 5.29 *cintāmaṇi-prakara-sadmasu*; 5.37 *ānanda-cinmaya-rasa-pratibhāvitābhis*; 5.40 *yasya prabhā prabhavato jagad-aṇḍa-koṭi*; 5.43 *tat parastād*). The commentary fixes the modern Gauḍīya reading of *Govinda* as the *ādi-puruṣa* (comm. on *BS* 5.1, identifying *ādi-puruṣaṃ tam ahaṃ bhajāmi* with Govinda-Kṛṣṇa) against any account that subordinates Kṛṣṇa to a more abstract *para-brahman*, and is therefore doctrinally complementary to the *Anubhāṣya* on the *acintya-śakti* side.

engaged_works.title: Vaishnava-Siddhanta-Mala
engaged_works.summary: A Bengali doctrinal compendium setting out the school's tenets in catechetical form (*Vaiṣṇava-Siddhānta-Mālā*, Gauḍīya Maṭha edition, prefatory remarks). The work is constructed for the Gauḍīya Maṭha's institutional pedagogy and supplies the standard answers to the principal doctrinal questions about *acintya-śakti* (sections on *bhagavat-tattva*), *prema-bhakti* (sections on *prema-tattva* and *rasa-tattva*), *vaiṣṇava-dīkṣā* (sections on *dīkṣā-tattva* and *guru-tattva*), the relation of the school to *advaita-vāda* (sections on *māyāvāda-khaṇḍana*), and the soteriological status of the *jīva* (sections on *jīva-tattva*). The treatise is the principal doctrinal handbook of the modern Gauḍīya Maṭha tradition and stands alongside the *Anubhāṣya* and the *Bhāgavata*-commentary in the school's twentieth-century reference apparatus. The treatment of *vaiṣṇava-dīkṣā* outside hereditary lineages is doctrinally consequential: it codifies the school's break with *jāti-vaiṣṇava* practice (*Vaiṣṇava-Siddhānta-Mālā*, *dīkṣā-tattva* section, citing Sanātana Gosvāmī, *Hari-Bhakti-Vilāsa* 2.4 *yathā kāñcanatāṃ yāti*) and grounds the modern institutional argument that *dīkṣā* is conditioned by qualification and surrender rather than by birth.

engaged_works.title: Editions and translations of the Gosvami corpus
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored editorial labour, distinguished from authorship of the underlying texts.
engaged_works.summary: Editions and translations of the Gauḍīya pre-modern corpus — Rūpa (*Bhakti-Rasāmṛta-Sindhu*; *Ujjvala-Nīlamaṇi*), Sanātana (*Bṛhad-Bhāgavatāmṛta*; *Hari-Bhakti-Vilāsa*), Jīva (*Ṣaṭ-Sandarbha*; *Gopāla-Campū*), Viśvanātha (*Sārārtha-Darśinī*), and Baladeva (*Govinda-Bhāṣya*) — published through the Gauḍīya Maṭha press (publication colophons, Gauḍīya Maṭha 1920–1937). The editorial labour is the principal documentary infrastructure on which the school's modern teaching tradition depends, and is doctrinally consequential because the editions and translations are themselves carriers of the school's reading. Bhaktisiddhānta's editorial practice consistently foregrounds the *Ṣaṭ-Sandarbha* doctrinal grid and the *acintya-śakti* metaphysic (cf. Bhaktisiddhānta's editorial introductions to the *Tattva-Sandarbha* and *Bhakti-Sandarbha* editions), and lets the pre-modern corpus speak in the school's modern doctrinal voice. The use of print-publication as the *bṛhad-mṛdaṅga* — the great drum of Hari-saṅkīrtana — is the institutional theology Bhaktisiddhānta articulates explicitly (Bhaktisiddhānta, editorials in *Gauḍīya* and *Sajjana-Toṣaṇī*, 1922–1935, programmatic statements on the press as *bṛhad-mṛdaṅga*).
